text: The gastrocolic trunk of Henle is a venous trunk of the tripod confluence consisting of the right gastropiploic vein, pancreatoduodenal superior anterior vein and the superior right colic vein. Normally, the ileocolic vein, middle right colic vein, pancreatoduodenal inferior anterior vein and the middle colic vein don't drain into the trunk of Henle. This tripod confluence is found in sixty percent of cases. In twenty percent of cases the trunk of Henle is made by two veins because the superior right colic vein drains directly into the superior mesenteric vein. In fifteen percent of cases the middle colic vein joins the trunk of Henle. Just in five percent of cases the iliocolic vein joins the trunk of Henle. The surgical area of the gastrocolic trunk of Henle was considered the area of the superior mesenteric vein located at the head of the pancreas, defined by the venous confluence of the three veins. The lympho-deposed tissue located in the medial border of the inferior mesenteric vein from the junction of the ileocolic vein with the superior mesenteric vein to the trunk of Henle is known as surgical trunk of Gilotte. The superior right colic vein exits and drains from the right colonic flexor to the trunk of Henle in ninety percent of cases. Dissection of the gastrocolic trunk of Henle and the surgical trunk of Gilotte are crucial steps to achieve a proper D-III lymphadenectomy and complete mesocolic excision.

In a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy on an obese male patient, the surgeon opens the mesenteric window at the inferior edge of the ileocolic vascular pedicle. The ileocolic vessels are identified and relegated. The inferior part of the omentum is exposed first, followed by the ansinate process of the pancreas. Lymph node dissection is performed along the superior mesenteric vein from the origin of the ileocolic vein to the gastrocolic trunk of Henle's surgical area.

The surgeon carefully exposes the superior right colic vein from the medial side, using it as an anatomical landmark for the oncological right hemicolectomy. Lymph nodes are dissected around the superior right colic vein and the right branch of the middle colic vessels, with attention to vascular variability and fragility to minimize intraoperative bleeding risks.

Images of the freshly excised surgical specimen are shown, highlighting the D-III lymphadenectomy area. The specimen includes the ascending colon with mucinous adenocarcinoma, and the retroperitoneal margin is free of cancer. Twenty-eight lymph nodes are visible, all negative for metastasis, including five from the D-III lymphadenectomy area.
